one_line_takeaway: Instead of biasing token sampling, fine-tune the LLM with RL so its weights carry a watermark that a paired neural detector reads off in one forward pass, yielding near-perfect clean detection, far lower false-positive rates than KGW/ITS/EXP, and (via adversarial training) strong robustness to substitution and paraphrasing.
key_finding: The co-trained model-level watermark achieves near-perfect clean detection and dramatically better false-positive rates than the token-level baselines at comparable or better utility. On OPT-1.3B C4, 'Ours' reaches AUC 0.9985 with FPR@99 0.9% vs KGW 0.9698/57.7% and EXP 0.9777/100.0%; on PKU alignment 'Ours' reaches AUC 0.9997 (FPR@99 0.4%) while EXP collapses to AUC 0.3224. The detector-only ablation 'Ours (No-FT)' is much weaker (e.g. PKU OPT AUC 0.9904 vs full 0.9997; and only 0.9820 clean AUC on C4 OPT), showing that FINE-TUNING the LLM, not just the neural detector, drives the gain. Under attacks, the plain method is not inherently robust to heavy substitution (e.g. Llama2 C4 AUC falls to 0.1562 at 50% substitution), but with adversarial training robustness is nearly fully restored (AUC ~1.0 even at 50% substitution). Against paraphrasing the method is already stronger than baselines even without adversarial training (e.g. OPT PKU Pegasus T=2.0 AUC 0.9585 vs KGW 0.6344, EXP 0.1550), and adversarial training on Pegasus transfers to unseen DIPPER paraphrasing.
core_contribution: The paper proposes a MODEL-LEVEL LLM watermarking scheme in which the watermark signal is embedded into the LLM's weights via fine-tuning, rather than into the token sampling at inference time (as in prior fixed-model methods). The key idea is a reinforcement-learning co-training loop that alternately (1) uses PPO to fine-tune the LLM so its outputs score high under a learned detector, and (2) trains the detector (a language-model-based reward model, D(x,y)) to separate watermarked from non-watermarked text. Unlike prior work whose detectors are simple statistical tests, the detector here is a neural reward-model-shaped classifier paired with the watermarked LLM. Claimed advantages over fixed-model approaches: higher detection accuracy, robustness to paraphrasing (because the signal is semantic/model-level rather than token-level), adaptability to new attacks via adversarial training, zero watermark-generation cost at inference, and feasibility of open-sourcing the watermarked model (no post-hoc step to hide). This is the authors' OWN contribution; the KGW/ITS/EXP statistical-test watermarks are cited baselines, and Gu et al. 2023 (fine-tuning to distill an inference-time watermark) is cited as the closest related work but distinguished because its detection is still statistical and it is often outperformed by KGW.
threat_model: The defender is an LLM service provider who develops and deploys an LLM and wants to reliably attribute generated text back to its own model in order to track misuse. The provider has the compute to fine-tune the LLM and can collect relevant fine-tuning data. Goal: given a text (prompt x, output y), decide whether it was produced by the provider's watermarked model versus any other source (human-written or a different LLM), with high true-positive rate and low false-positive rate, while preserving normal utility. Adversaries attempt to erase/evade the watermark via (a) random word/token substitution and (b) paraphrasing (Pegasus, DIPPER). The paper also briefly discusses (in Discussion, not evaluated) resistance to spoofing attacks, arguing that because the watermark is data-driven and not an algorithm transparent to the attacker, it may be harder to forge.
limitations: Author-acknowledged: (1) one-time fine-tuning cost makes the overall watermark more expensive than fixed-model methods (mitigated only if folded into standard alignment); (2) it is data-driven, so practitioners must collect relevant fine-tuning data; (3) detection is costlier (a neural forward pass vs a cheap statistical test). Reviewer-observed from the paper's own numbers: the plain method is NOT robust to strong token substitution without adversarial training (Llama2 C4 drops to 0.1562 at 50% substitution), i.e. robustness is largely an artifact of retraining against the specific attack rather than intrinsic. Detection on PKU/Llama2-7B is comparatively weak (authors attribute it to only 5K RLHF samples for the larger model). Cross-source generalization degrades: a model fine-tuned only on human text (Ours(H)) drops to AUC 0.9053 when tested on another LLM's text (Table 2, C4/OPT), recovered only by adding that LLM's text to training (Ours(H+L)). Spoofing resistance and detector-transfer across model updates are only discussed, not evaluated. Experiments are limited to two models up to 7B and two tasks; no comparison to Gu et al.'s learned/distilled watermark despite it being the closest prior work.

datasets_used: PKU SafeRLHF / BeaverTails (safety-alignment Q&A; safety scored with the released beaver-7b cost/safety model)
Paraphrasers used as attackers: Pegasus and DIPPER
quantitative_results: OPT-1.3B C4: Ours AUC 0.9985, FPR@90 0.1%, FPR@99 0.9%, logPPL 2.4177 (best/lowest PPL of all methods)
OPT-1.3B PKU: Ours AUC 0.9997, FPR@90 0.0%, FPR@99 0.4%, Safety Score 10.73
Llama2-7B C4: Ours AUC 0.9989, FPR@90 0.0%, FPR@99 0.1%, logPPL 8.2997
Llama2-7B PKU: Ours AUC 0.9947, FPR@90 0.7%, FPR@99 3.8%, Safety Score 2.698
Baseline collapse on PKU: EXP AUC 0.3224 (OPT) and 0.7370 (Llama2); KGW PKU FPR@99 81.8% (OPT)
Ablation Ours(No-FT): OPT C4 AUC 0.9820 (FPR@99 34.6%) and OPT PKU AUC 0.9904 vs full Ours 0.9985/0.9997
Substitution robustness (Table 7): plain Ours degrades badly at ratio 0.5 (Llama2 C4 AUC 0.1562; OPT PKU 0.5764), but Ours+AdvTrain ~1.0 across ratios (OPT PKU 1.0 at 0.5)
Pegasus paraphrase T=2.0 (Table 8): OPT PKU Ours 0.9585 vs KGW 0.6344, EXP 0.1550; Ours+AdvTrain 0.9925
DIPPER paraphrase q=60 (Table 9): OPT C4 Ours 0.9431 vs KGW 0.6105; Ours+AdvTrain 0.9913
Cross-LLM detection (Table 2): Ours(H) C4/OPT drops to Test-L AUC 0.9053; Ours(H+L) recovers to 0.9994
baselines_compared: KGW (Kirchenbauer et al. 2023a) - green/red vocabulary-split inference-time watermark
ITS (Kuditipudi et al. 2023) - distortion-free pre-set-key inference-time sampling watermark
EXP (Kuditipudi et al. 2023) - key-based sampling-probability-adjustment watermark
Ours (No-FT) - own ablation: train detector only, no LLM fine-tuning
